0.0%

Money Market Fund

4.0%

Cash

0.9%

The fact that the fund lists a 0% allocation to both rights and warrants does not mean that it does not have any of these securities in its portfolio. In fact, the fund has a fairly large number of these securities issued by a number of gold and silver mining companies:

We even see some warrants in here from companies such as Bunker Hill Mining Company (OTCQB:BHLL), whose primary asset is a silver, lead, and zinc mine located in Idaho. Ridgeline Minerals (OTC:RDGMF) adds another metal into the mix, as one of its projects is located in an area that has proven to be high in copper, silver, lead, and zinc. For the most part, though, we can see that the majority of the fund’s warrants are for companies that primarily produce gold and silver.

The fund’s common stock portfolio also consists primarily of gold and silver mining companies. The annual report provides the following sector breakdown as of November 30, 2024:

Sector

% of Net Assets

Gold mining, exploration, development and royalty companies

64.7%

Diversified metals mining, exploration, development and royalty companies

27.6%

Silver mining, exploration, development and royalty companies

1.2%

We see that the fund is focused mostly on gold, which makes a lot of sense given that gold has generally been performing better than other precious metals. We can see this quite clearly by looking at a three-year price chart:

Here is an index for the chart:

Metal

Color

Platinum

Blue

Palladium

Dark Purple

Silver

Orange

Gold

Green

We can clearly see that gold has performed the best over the past three years by a fairly considerable margin. Palladium was obviously the worst performer, dropping 60.99% over the three-year period. Silver and platinum were both in the middle, with silver outperforming platinum. Thus, gold and silver were the two best performers over the three-year period, so it does make a certain amount of sense that the fund would opt to focus its attention on owning companies with exposure to those two metals as opposed to including a company such as Anglo American Platinum (OTCPK:ANGPY), the largest platinum miner in the world, in its portfolio.

ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited also has a respectable amount of international exposure. While the fund itself is Bermudan, it invests in companies from a number of different countries. Here are the fund’s company holdings by country as of November 30, 2024:

Country Name

% of Net Assets

Australia

18.5%

Canada

69.7%

Cayman Islands

1.8%

South Africa

1.6%

United Kingdom

1.5%

United States

1.8%

ASA Gold and Precious Metals has one of the lowest weightings to the United States of any fund that I have ever seen, which could actually be pretty appealing to investors in certain circumstances. Despite the recent weakness in the United States compared to the rest of the world, that country still accounts for 70.81% of the MSCI World Index (URTH):

As such, many investors, and especially those based in the United States, likely have substantial exposure to the United States across their portfolios. It is always risky to have an outsized proportion of your portfolio exposed to only a single country, as we have seen year-to-date. After all, the S&P 500 Index has underperformed both the MSCI Europe (IEUR) and the MSCI Japan (EWJ) indices. It can be a good idea to spread your assets around the world in order to reduce portfolio volatility in such an environment and the fact that ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited only has very limited exposure to the American stock market could help an investor accomplish this goal.

ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited is also underweight to the United States when compared to some of the indices tracking the gold mining industry. One of the more popular index funds tracking this sector is the Van Eck Gold Miners ETF (GDX), which tracks the NYSE Arca Gold Miners Index. That index currently has a 15.99% weighting to the United States:

Thus, ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited is substantially underweight the United States compared to the index. The fund is also substantially overweight Canada and Australia compared to the index. This may not be a bad thing, depending on the composition of the remainder of the portfolio.

The fact that ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited has a different composition than the gold mining index has not hurt its performance. In fact, it appears to have helped it as this fund has beaten the NYSE Arca Gold Miners Index over the past ten years on a total return basis:

While past performance is no guarantee of future results, this is a pretty good track record that shows that the fund’s management has expertise in choosing those companies that are going to succeed. The mining industry in general is one in which active management can be more helpful than a passive strategy due to the expense and uncertainty involved in the development of a new mine. By choosing to invest in companies with the best assets, this fund has managed to provide very good returns for its shareholders over time.

Gold Price Catalyst

ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited has the majority of its assets invested in gold miners, so obviously, gold prices are much more important than the prices of silver or other precious metals for predicting the future performance of this fund. Fortunately, there are several reasons to believe that gold prices will be going higher from here.

One of the reasons for this is that historically gold has acted as a store of value that protects investors against inflation. Forbes Advisor states:

Many investors consider gold to be the ultimate safe-haven hedge against inflation. It’s been a store of value for thousands of years, and it has real-world uses in jewelry and electronics, which provides tangible value. And unlike fiat currencies, there is a relatively limited supply of gold.

However, Forbes then goes on to explain that gold’s record is somewhat mixed when it comes to its ability to provide protection against inflation. Forbes does have a point, as gold can be fairly volatile in the short term. As we saw earlier in this article, gold is up 54.94% over the past three years, and while there may be reasons to believe that the Consumer Price Index understates inflation, I doubt that anyone believes that most products today are 54.94% higher in price than they were three years ago. Thus, it seems likely that gold has overshot inflation over the trailing three-year period.

However, gold does work pretty well to preserve the purchasing power of wealth over the long term. For example, according to CNBC, in 1940, the median home value in the United States was $2,938. The average price of gold in 1940 was $34.50 per ounce. Thus, the median house cost about 85 ounces of gold. In 1980, the median house was priced at $47,200, which was about 77 ounces of gold, given the average closing price of $612.80 per ounce in that year. As of right now, the spot price of gold is $3,031.00 per ounce. Visual Capitalist states that the median house price in the United States is approximately $385,000. This actually works out to 127 ounces of gold at today’s spot price. We can see then that gold is not a perfect store of wealth, but it certainly does better than the U.S. dollar (and by extension U.S. dollars sitting in some sort of cash-equivalent security).

We can see further evidence that gold provides certain protections against inflation by looking at a gold price chart that has been adjusted for inflation. This chart shows the average price of gold in every month since June 1915, adjusted for inflation using the Consumer Price Index:

The inflation adjustment puts the average price of gold in June 1915 at $614.23 per ounce compared to $3,123.82 per ounce on average in March 2025. That is, therefore, a 508.5% increase in gold prices over the period. If the Consumer Price Index is an accurate measure of inflation, and if gold were a perfect inflation hedge, then the change in the inflation-adjusted gold price would be zero over the entire period. Thus, gold did appear to be volatile over the period, but it still did much better at acting as a store of wealth than did U.S. dollars.

This alone suggests that over the long term, holding gold is preferable to holding cash in a bank account or a money market fund. After all, one of the things that we all want is to maintain the purchasing power of the money that we work hard to earn, and gold appears to do a better job at this than cash savings.

Another reason to have gold in a portfolio today is as a hedge against volatility and market uncertainty. Gold historically does pretty well as a safe-haven asset in times of uncertainty. For example, gold prices rose during the late-2000s financial crisis and the recession that followed. Over the three-year period from January 1, 2007 to January 1, 2010, the spot price of gold rose by 71.27%:

Furthermore, during the eurozone debt crisis, which is generally considered to have occurred over the period that lasted from 2009 until the mid-2010s, gold prices rose significantly. This chart shows the spot price of gold from January 1, 2009 through January 1, 2014:

We can see that over this period, gold prices rose by 37.59%. Some readers may note that gold prices started to decline beginning in late 2012, which is true, however, they never declined much below the January 2014 level. As late as January 1, 2017, gold prices were still 31.45% above the level at which they stood at the start of 2009:

We can still clearly see that gold prices did pretty well in response to the eurozone debt crisis. Gold did start to give up some of its gains as the crisis was averted, but it never went back to even. This may have been due to the highly stimulative monetary policy that continued throughout the period, as gold is a store of value in addition to benefiting from the safe haven trade. Thus, gold will never go to zero and should always help to protect an investor.

The fact that gold benefits in times of uncertainty could actually work pretty well today. The market was shocked on Wednesday when the Trump Administration announced a series of tariffs, which has resulted in a two-day sell-off. Gold itself fell a bit on both days, which is somewhat surprising given its use as a safe haven play, but that could be due to traders getting margin calls and needing to sell everything indiscriminately in an effort to raise cash. Ultimately, though, it is unclear how this whole situation will play out as the administration has stated that it is open to reducing or even removing the tariffs depending on the actions of other countries. For example, Zero Hedge reports that a post by President Trump on social media this morning immediately caused stock futures to jump just because the administration admits that it would be willing to lift the tariffs on Vietnam if the Vietnamese government agrees to work out a trade deal with the United States.

Thus, there still appears to be a great deal of uncertainty and volatility in the market, which would normally be a great environment for a safe haven trade. Gold could work reasonably well as a way to protect assets over the long term as well as ride out the current uncertain environment. As the share price of ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited exhibits a very high correlation to gold prices, it could be an excellent option right now.

Valuation

Shares of ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited are currently trading at an 11.89% discount, which is slightly worse than its three-year average. Over the past three years, the fund has traded at discounts of 4.77% to 18.45%, with a 13.41% average.

While this fund is trading at slightly worse than average right now, the current discount seems like a reasonable price given that it is still a massive discount on the actual value of the underlying assets.

Expense Ratio

ASA Gold and Precious Metals has an expense ratio of 1.64%. The fund does not use leverage.

Saba Capital Proxy Battle

As of March 31, 2025, Saba Capital (an activist management company that tries to institute changes at closed-end funds in an attempt to reduce discounts) controlled 17.18% of ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited. Saba Capital has stated that it wants to make changes to the Board of Directors and the fund itself to try and reduce the size of the discount. However, as of today, Saba Capital has not delineated or proposed what changes it wants the fund to make.

This is speculation on my part, but one possible change would be increasing the distribution and possibly the frequency of it. This would likely attract investors who are looking for income. However, I am not too sure that this would be a good thing for the fund. As we have seen, this fund has historically been able to keep up very well with gold itself and beat the gold mining index. Its ability to do that could be reduced if it starts to pay out most or all of its realized gains since it will reduce the fund’s capital and limit its ability to invest in new opportunities. It would also increase the tax liability of all of the fund’s investors, which is not particularly desirable. However, at the moment, this is all speculation and the fund’s management team states that Saba Capital has thus far not proposed any actual changes.

In response to the move by Saba Capital, the fund will be distributing rights to all of its shareholders as of April 9, 2025. These rights will allow the shareholders to purchase one newly printed share of ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited for every share that the investor owns on April 9. However, these rights cannot be exercised unless someone either acquires 15% of the fund or Saba Capital attempts to increase its stake by more than 0.25% of the fund’s total outstanding shares. Basically, this appears to be an attempt to prevent anyone from seizing control of the fund without the approval of the majority of the shareholders.

At the moment, it is unclear what will come of this, but the rights distribution is intended to prevent anyone from really changing the fund’s strategy or the basic profile, so this does not change my thesis at this time.

Conclusion

In conclusion, a good case can be made for including some gold exposure in a portfolio today, especially for long-term investors seeking to maintain the purchasing of their assets. It could also be a good asset to hold right now, given the incredible uncertainty surrounding the global trade and the effects that the proposed tariffs and trade wars can have on the U.S. economy. ASA Gold and Precious Metals is an interesting closed-end fund that invests in mining companies, and not physical gold specifically, but its historical performance has been pretty good compared to gold itself.

ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited fund has slightly outperformed gold over time, and it has also outperformed gold mining indices, so it might be a good way to get exposure. The downside here is that the fund’s yield is quite low, so income investors may not be happy. It should work pretty well as an alternative to physical gold, though, and at least it provides a very small yield.

data.title: Mitsubishi Corporation: The Good And The Bad (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
My view of Mitsubishi Corporation (OTCPK:MTSUY) (OTCPK:MSBHF) (NEOE:MTSU:CA) (8058:JP) is Neutral now.

I was previously bullish on the stock. The September 23, 2024, article touched on the positives associated with its cash distributions and divestitures.

MSBHF is no longer a Buy, as it's likely to witness lower FY25 (YE March 31, 2026) earnings. But a Sell rating is unwarranted, considering MTSUY's ROE improvement potential and Berkshire Hathaway Inc.'s (BRK.B) (BRK.A) increased equity interest.

Unfavorable Near-Term Outlook Could Cap Share Price Upside

I anticipate that MTSU's net profit will decline by -22% to ¥ 750B in the current fiscal year.

My pessimistic financial forecasts for the company are supported by a number of factors.

MTSUY's 9MFY24 bottom line was boosted by around JPY 0.3 trillion of non-recurring items as per my estimates. It sold stakes in certain F&B (" Food & Beverage") businesses and two coal mines during that period. There was also a valuation gain resulting from a change in the accounting method for one of its investments ("convenience chain operator Lawson"). It's uncertain if a similar level of one-off income can be realized in FY 2025.

Assets Owned By Mitsubishi's "Mineral Resources" Or "MS" Segment

Also, the commodity down-cycle will likely hurt MSBHF's results for this year. Its MS division contributed over a quarter of the company's net income for the nine-month period ending on Dec 31, 2024. The average price of natural resources is projected to decline by -7% in '25-26 combined.

Another key business for MTSU is its Mobility unit, which represents a low-teens percentage of its earnings. This segment operates automotive operations in Thailand and Indonesia under the "Isuzu" and "Mitsubishi" brands, respectively. These two Southeast Asian nations have both been slapped with US tariffs at the 30+ percent level on "Liberation Day". This might have a negative impact on vehicle demand in these markets.

Stocks tend to perform well in terms of price appreciation and multiple re-rating when their earnings continue to go up. The reverse is true, and that's bad news for Mitsubishi Corporation. I think that MSBHF's shares won't rise substantially when it is expected to register negative growth in the short term.

But ROE Expansion And Buffett's Stake Addition Might Be Intermediate-Term Catalysts

If you are willing to take a longer-term view, the picture for MTSUY looks better.

On April 3, 2025, MTSU unveiled its goal of driving up its ROE from 10.4% in the prior fiscal year to more than 12.0% in fiscal 2027. My take is that this is achievable, as the company has many levers.

One ROE-boosting measure that the company could adopt is to increase its financial leverage. Its net gearing was reasonably comfortable at 0.42 times. MSBHF has set a comparatively higher net debt-to-equity ceiling of 60% for the subsequent three-year period.

It is also making its equity base smaller to enhance ROE. MTSUY is targeting to distribute at least JPY 2,400 billion of capital to its shareholders via repurchase and dividends between FY25 and FY27. This is equivalent to a meaningful 24% of the stock's net assets.

MTSU Is Making Tweaks To Individual Businesses

MSBHF has plans to boost its overall earnings by improving the profit margins of certain divisions as detailed above.

A superior ROE will enable Mitsubishi to command a more demanding valuation, and I'm confident that will happen in the future.

Separately, BRK.B has raised its stake in MTSU from 8.91% earlier to 9.67% (Source: S&P Capital IQ) in mid-March this year.

Buffett mentioned in his recent letter that he has the intention to increase Berkshire's interest in the major Japanese conglomerates (including MTSUY) to above 10 percent in due course. This could be a multiple expansion trigger for the medium term.

Many people could possibly mimic BRK.A's portfolio actions and buy more shares of MTSU. There's nothing compared to getting an endorsement from a well-respected investing legend. This has positive read-throughs for MSBHF's future stock price trajectory.

The Final Word

I arrived at a target P/B metric of 1.2x for Mitsubishi, and that's close to its current P/NAV multiple of 1.14x as per S&P Capital IQ. The limited +5% ("1.2/1.14") potential upside is supportive of my Hold rating.

The GGM ("Gordon Growth Model") Valuation Model

My ROE, k ("cost of equity"), and g ("perpetuity growth rate") inputs for the formulae presented above are 12% ("company's 2027 target"), 10%, and 0, respectively. I have assumed a higher k (versus the market's 9 percent) and zero growth to factor in the company's net income contraction for FY25.

In my opinion, both the good ("medium-term re-rating triggers") and the bad ("poor short-term prospects") are already reflected in MSBHF's price. I have decided to downgrade my rating for MTSU to a Hold, considering its valuation and outlook.

data.title: Penguin Solutions Earnings: Tariff Risks, But A Stronger Balance Sheet
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Penguin Solutions, Inc. (NASDAQ:PENG) was unlucky to report its Q2 2025 earnings results on the back of the Liberation Day announcement, and all the market uncertainty brought on by the newly imposed tariffs.

Every investor wants a bargain. But bargains only come out when the outlook is grim. There are no other times when stocks go on sale. And right now, I believe we can all agree: the outlook is the worst it has been for a long time.

Penguin Solutions competes against the likes of Super Micro Computer, Inc. (SMCI). And Penguin Solutions' guidance gives us some early insights into how companies are navigating this period.

It was not a blemish-free report, but I believe that its valuation is already beaten down enough that it offers investors a compelling risk-reward at 9x forward free cash flow.

Rapid Recap -- My Bad Call

Back in January, I said,

[...] while there are some important blemishes to keep in mind, I believe there's enough of a positive setup to this name, to take its share price higher in the medium term.

With the benefit of hindsight, that was the wrong call to make on PENG. I had a lot of hopes for PENG, but this business has simply struggled to make a successful turnaround.

Penguin Solutions Near-Term Prospects

Penguin Solutions helps big companies and cloud providers build high-performance computing systems.

They seek to make it easy to set up AI infrastructure by offering a mix of hardware and services that ensure reliability.

Whether it's helping with power or advanced computing needs, they focus on delivering hardware and software that maximize performance.

During the earnings call, they made the case that looking ahead, Penguin Solutions is in a strong position as AI adoption ramps up in big businesses.

That said, the overwhelming interest for investors right now is the potential impact of tariffs—especially on their LED business.

Management sought to make clear that they've had challenges before, and while it may see some gross margin pressure, they are adapting to the news flow as is everyone else.

With that in mind, let's now discuss its fundamentals.

Revenue Growth Rates Get Upwards Revised

It's very challenging to report your earnings in this period. It reminds me of Adobe Inc. (ADBE) being one of the first companies to report during the Covid peak.

Investors clung tightly to the idea that you should sell first, and ask questions later. Negativity drops share prices down quickly. And slowly, stocks rise in steps.

For their part, Penguin raised their fiscal 2025 outlook by around 200 basis points, from 15% y/y to 17% y/y revenue growth rates.

However, right now, whether it's good news or bad news investors are treating nearly all small caps with the same brush.

Given this background, let's now discuss its valuation.

PENG Stock Valuation -- 9x Forward Free Cash Flow

Penguin Solutions ended fiscal Q2 2025 with a roughly net-neutral balance sheet. This is a significant improvement in its balance sheet, in part driven by its strong cash generation in the quarter.

However, we must keep in mind that Penguin Solutions raised more than $190 million of cash from the proceeds of its convertible debt. This means that this convertible debt must pay around 6% in annual dividends to the debt holders, and they convert into ordinary shares at $32.81 per share, at least 70% higher than the current share price (page 20).

Put simply, in the quarter, there was a capital raise, and so its market cap carries $200 million of convertible debt, that isn't immediately obvious from its balance sheet, as it's recorded as additional paid-in capital:

The highlighted part in the balance sheet above is for fiscal Q2 2025. This means that the company will need to tackle this convertible debt before there are any considerations towards shareholder capital returns.

Moving on, for H1 2025, Penguin delivered a 30.8% non-GAAP gross margin. However, its newly updated guidance now points to 31% plus or minus 1%, down from its prior guidance of 32% plus or minus 1%.

Even though Penguin didn't make clear that it's getting meaningfully impacted by tariffs, they did state that further than expected growth in its hardware sales, as well as faster growth in its lower-margin Memory business, is leading to some compression in its gross margin profile.

Furthermore, given the particularly strong free cash flow quarter just reported, I believe that in fiscal 2025, Penguin Solutions may well end up delivering more than $110 million of free cash flow.

As a reference point, keep in mind that Penguin Solutions reported just over $82 million of free cash flow for H1 2025.

If my $110 million free cash flow estimate is vaguely right, this would be a more than 90% increase in free cash flow from fiscal 2024.

Altogether, I believe that paying approximately 9x forward free cash flow for PENG already prices in a lot of negativity.

Risk Factors to Consider

These are wild and unpredictable times. Even though, thus far, we've not heard the chorus of supply chain disruption, I cannot imagine a scenario where this doesn't become a major theme in the next weeks and months.

This could mean that Penguin does reach my estimated $110 million of free cash flow figure for fiscal 2025, but after that, in fiscal 2026, its free cash flow fully dries up.

On top of that, while they have strong relationships with key players like Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL) and SK Telecom Co., Ltd. (SKM), they’re up against major companies like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (HPE), and Super Micro, which are also determined to take market share in AI-focused computing solutions.

The Bottom Line

At 9x forward free cash flow, Penguin Solutions presents a compelling investment opportunity, especially in a market environment where small-cap stocks are being indiscriminately punished.

Despite macroeconomic challenges, the company has strengthened its cash position, revised revenue growth upwards to 17%, and continues to generate robust free cash flow.

According to my estimates, Penguin Solutions may potentially exceed $110 million for fiscal 2025, a nearly 90% increase from last year.

While concerns around tariffs and competition remain, PENG’s strong positioning in the AI infrastructure space could be attractive beyond the near-term noise.

data.title: Boston Omaha: Undervalued Gem Or Dangerous Investment
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

In this article, I'll be looking at a sub-$500M market cap business, but one that has received (and rightly so) quite a bit of attention from many investors over the past few years. This is one of those almost owner-operated businesses that operates in a very high-moat area (one of them), and in several others that don't seem to have much correlation with the former. It was originally known as REO Plus, but changed its name to Boston-Omaha about 9 years ago.

The main appeal to anyone investing in the company is the 'moat-worthiness' and the entry barriers of that advertising segment, which focuses on managing over 4,000 billboard structures with about 7,500+ faces, the revenue of which has high regulatory barriers due to how things like this are managed in the US.

Boston Omaha is unrated; it cannot be viewed in terms of P/E (because the P/E is negative, and the company's current price trades at -266x P/E), and we instead have to view the company through a different lens. The company also comes with a very low leverage of below 15% long-term debt to capital.

In this article, I will provide a general overview, but also give you my take on whether you should invest in the company, and if you do, what you should expect the company to "give you" in return.

Boston Omaha from A to Z - Introducing the company.

So, as mentioned, the company has a number of different business interests. While each of them can be considered attractive in their own right, the more relevant are the Billboard business, Link, the Broadband company "BOB" and the general Indemnity group, known as GIG.

The insurance company, which was actually what initially aroused my interest to the company, offers commercial-line P&C insurance — fairly straightforward, with operations in 50 states and providing so-called surety bonds that in fact are very attractive as part of the business as well, because they are necessary to cover commercial projects and government contracts. For those not schooled in the vernacular of Insurance, what exactly are surety bonds?

A surety bond is a contract between three parties where one Party A guarantees B that C will perform according to the bond statutes. So, compared to a more standard contract, which has two parties, this has three. Because the parties involved often involve government parties, these are not unattractive in any way.

These three segments make up Boston-Omaha's main operations, but the company also has minority investments in other sectors which are not unattractive, such as the company investment in Sky Harbour, which is an aviation development company that owns private hangars and thereto-related services for aviation assets and the like. It's a publicly traded company under the symbol SKYH. The company also has investments in things like Banks, which, unfortunately, haven't performed all that well.

But this is the sort of company that requires not only basic research but also a significant understanding of how to value a company overall. This is a "long-term" play. The company also had a recent transition to a new CEO (different from more than one previously), and the plan here has been clear — a focus on billboards, broadband, and insurance.

The company has, without a doubt, been through a fair bit of internal turmoil. I would argue, however, that this turmoil doesn't show in its share price, which, over 6-8 months, has been relatively stable.

The first question should be what we can expect from this holding company's main assets on a forward basis. This is, after all, the safety of what you're buying when you're purchasing shares on the market. The company believes its shares to be undervalued — this is confirmed by the fact that it has initiated a $20M program for share buybacks, which for a company with less than half a billion in market cap is very significant.

Much of the recent turmoil has been due to events in 2024. The company lost its co-CEO, Mr. Rozek, who not only left but also sold every single share to his name in BOH. To anyone following the business, there were signs that something wasn't right, including a very sudden decision to wind down its asset management and also change the form of its AGM to virtual. This alone would have been enough for me to consider an exit without an explanation being provided. However, following what the company did after the co-CEO left, I was encouraged by the company's focus on its core assets. There was also a whole calculation, since the shares that were sold by the Co-CEO were shares with exponentially larger voting power than the common shares — and which were then sold at a total of almost $20M, which the company was forced to buy back from the leaving co-CEO. That amounted to almost 5% of the company's market cap and a premium of over 50% to the common share price at the time (Source: Seeking Alpha Boston Omaha Valuation)

You might be asking where the value could be in such a business, and it's a fair question. To find this value, we need to look at the individual businesses in their own right.

The billboard company is probably by far the most attractive in my estimate. It has extremely stable overall revenues due to government restrictions on new billboards. These rules mean that you cannot grow a new billboard company organically. It's legally impossible. You can also see above in the filing/presentation that the company is one of the fewer exceptionally profitable ones that this company owns and has been for a while. These combinations make this asset very hard to value (on the high side). The company is currently generating about $20M in FCF a year on this and are in the process of converting these billboards to digital assets.

Meanwhile, the rural internet company is probably one of the most uncertain companies that BOC has. I know some love the idea, and I'm on board with the importance of fiber cable connections, even for rural areas. The appeal I see comes in the form of government grants; however, what the current administration thinks of such grants when there are things like Starlink and other satellite internet providers, we will have to see. I'm unsure of the long-term appeal of this one in the end, but I remain cautiously optimistic due to the growth-oriented nature and underpenetrated market this near-$200M company works in, now consisting of InfoWest, Aiebeam, Utah Broadband, and others. You can also see above that despite the losses, these have slimmed down, and the company has excellent gross margins and is expecting profitability going forward.

The GIG business has done well meanwhile, growing its written premium both through causality and surety by 40%, earned premiums by 42%, and net income by 32% — all while maintaining a very attractive loss ratio. I view the insurance business as far more interesting than the broadband business at this time and in these political circumstances — and management in BOC seems to agree, since 3 months ago, the company added capital to the insurance subsidiary to fund more growth (this addition was in the form of Sky Harbor Stock). You can also see how the GIG segment or business is one of the other net-income positives of BOC, which is good.

Sky Harbor is a bit of a "special case". The company's stake in the company can be valued at about $200M, given its about 20% stake and the current $1B market cap — though you will note it's quite a volatile stock. Also, valuing it at market cap also needs to come with the disclaimer that Sky Harbor is not a profitable business — and you know, if you follow my work, what I think about unprofitable businesses. It has an annual revenue of less than $15M and a GAAP EPS of -$1.76. Even considering this one at market cap is done only at the risk of the performance numbers. Seeking Alpha gives the company a "D" in profitability due to these factors. (Source)

So, you can see that BOC is a "collection" of stocks and holdings that together make up the holding business, a majority stake of which is in the hands of the current CEO.

The company does not pay a dividend. Other risks for the company include whether the growth in insurance is sustainable, given the current macro climate, as well as the headwinds I mentioned for broadband. I personally do not believe billboard advertisements will ever become "unattractive" per se, so I don't see this as a risk.

Turning to the positives, of which there are several. The company's share buybacks imply that, yes, the company is viewed as undervalued by management. They would not spend 3-5% of market cap on buybacks otherwise. The company is developing several attractive businesses, and the recent changes in leadership seem to be working out for them.

However, all of this has not helped investors — yet.

Boston Omaha — What is the upside, and in what timeframe?

Because while all of these things can even be considered somewhat compelling, what exactly have investors made from the company?

Given the zero dividend, investors during the last five years have made negative returns.

This is during a time when my own returns have been above 180%. That's better than the DJIA, it's better than every single index I have been comparing it to. This is for my entire portfolio, of course, but it is worth mentioning when comparing these things. You can confirm this performance below.

The company fully admits that its returns have been sub-par in terms of investor return since the inception of the stock. While, in the company's own words, "this is not abnormal", and while historical returns are not an indicator of future ones, I would still be very cautious here.

The lack of a dividend means that you're not getting anything beyond the company's share price performance — and because that has been terrible (5-year negative returns in the double digits in a market when the Nasdaq is up 150% is indeed 'terrible'), the company needs "arguments" for an investment here.

Some analysts would argue that valuing the company's component at market prices (for sale), you would very quickly get to the current market cap of the company. And you would. The SKYH stake alone is $200M, nearly half the company's market cap (provided you actually view this market cap as valid, of course, given its lack of profit), and even just valuing the other stakes/holdings essentially at 1x revenue based on 2024 numbers, you're not that far from the company's current market cap — and of course BOC can be considered worth more than 1x revenue of its solid billboard, broadband and insurance company.

But how exactly do we discount for this? The company isn't selling these stakes, and I don't see monetization or yield for SKYH as likely either, so arguing they "should" be valued there is kind of like arguing the company you own a stake in with your majority-owning brother should be worth "this much". It might be true, but it's unlikely you'll find a buyer at the price for the stake.

It's too simplistic.

BOC is potentially attractive, yes. It is. But it doesn't "pay you", and monetization in a way that shareholders might enjoy seems ways off. The fact is, the positive articles for this company have a history — and a long one. But keep in mind, the company has never really gone anywhere. The initial share price was close to $13.19 in 2017. Now it's $14.69. That's not really growth worth an 8-year period.

That this company is fundamentally undervalued is an argument that can be made. But unlike any of the large caps I typically cover, where catalysts for reversal or growth don't matter as much given the yield or the foundational safety of a multi-billion dollar business, this company has none of that.

You might be positive on the company, but I would like to remind you, if you are, that anyone who's had a bearish view on this company since 2023 or before has typically made the right choice — at least for now.

What could be catalysts for the business? Remember that BOC used to trade at a discount even when large parts of book value were cash. It's also clear that the CEO's are not exactly short of cash, despite shareholders being left with zero following their work here. Although the agreement from several years ago seems to be different now, it's important to remember that those co-CEOs got around $7.5M cash each every year, due to using book value as the metric to track performance and incentive back during the MIBP, the Management Incentive Bonus Plan (Source). To quote from the 23/24 annual filing, page 10 (emphasis mine):

On February 27, 2018, the Compensation Committee approved changes to the MIBP, effected through an amendment and restatement of the MIBP, including placing certain caps on the total payments under the MIBP through December 2032 and additional annual caps thereafter, as well as establishing a high water mark under the MIBP so that any decrease in adjusted stockholders’ equity per share in any prior year must be first recouped before the 6% hurdle test is applied. Previously, there were no caps on the amounts payable under the MIBP. In accordance with the terms of the MIBP, each of Messrs. Rozek and Peterson earned a $7,500,000 bonus for 2021 based upon the increase in stockholders’ equity in 2021 (excluding increases in stockholders’ equity per share resulting from issuances by the Company of its securities or securities of any subsidiary for cash consideration) which exceeded 106% of our stockholders’ equity for the preceding fiscal year.

(Source: 10-K)

Well, to shareholders, book value doesn't mean much if it's not at some point reflected in the actual stock price, which it has not been. To be absolutely clear, I'm in no way against compensating executive management well — but when done in this fashion as it was, it's a bit of a "needle" in the eye of a stockholder that has been getting essentially nothing. Also, I would like to remind you that together with the co-CEO salaries, those bonuses of $15M together made up in comparison to 2024 results 7.7x the net income of the entire company for the year of 2024. That's with a $2.1M net income, accounting for the net losses by the various segments.

You may note, dear readers, that I typically do not dig as deeply as this in companies when I research them. But when it's about a small company such as this that isn't under the same sort of media and regulatory scrutiny as an IG-rated huge business, doing this sort of due diligence is paramount.

In the end, when I look at this company, I look at my portfolio. I am getting a 4.8% average yield on cash that I put into my collection of attractively valued, dividend-paying stocks.

Do I think that BOC's collection of admittedly attractive assets will outperform the market in the same way that would justify the share price moving this way in that timeframe and potentially reward me as an investor?

Maybe.

Do I think the chance of that offsets the risk of investing in this no-dividend stock?

No, I don't. Not even at this price.

A trap that value investors can fall into when looking at companies, especially smaller ones, is to think that they have found an "undervalued gem" that the market just doesn't know how to value or has ignored for too long. It's entirely possible to do this.

However, it's equally impossible to miss something as the value investor. I have missed things in billion-euro companies - so why shouldn't it be even more possible for someone to miss something in the case of a less-than-$500M company?

Or, let's say that you haven't missed something, but the market sentiment just doesn't agree with you. Because your investment has no yield, that means your returns are dictated by market sentiment, i.e, share price. You need catalysts that the market agrees with.

My point is this.

Even at a price that I consider 35% discounted at least to the realistic value of the company, I do not consider this enough of an offset to the risk taken if you invest in this instead of a 4-7% yielding A-rated business that is out there at a historical discount today.

So, with respect to my title for the article, I say that both are true. Boston Omaha is an undervalued gem — but it's also a dangerous investment.

It's my view that beating the market can be done without going into the small and micro-caps. I've done it for almost every year running so far.

I would, in fact, value the company at revenue multiple 1x for the smaller segments and at current market for the SKYH stake. 2 analysts follow the company with an average PT of $15-$28/share, which puts their valuation on the high side to $811M. (Source: Paywalled TIKR.com link). Finding a peer to try and value the company here is near impossible -there are no peers with the same mix. My 1x revenue multiple with SKYH holding valuation comes to a per-share value of $10.74 based on 28.9M shares. I will round that up to $11.

That means the company would have to drop back down to very low levels for me to even consider being interested here. In fact, the company has never been that low. But it gives you an indication of how I discount when I look at companies.

Remember that in order for BOC investments to make sense to me, they not only have to show potential, but they also have to measure up against every other investment I can make at the time. And the company simply does not — not at this price, at any rate.

To conclude, I will simply say that the broader market has had one development since its inception back in 2018. The S&P 500 is up 112.38% since the earliest SA article on this stock. The stock, though, is down 35.29% — and despite this, many analysts have remained positive throughout this entire journey.

I will follow the company and look for catalysts materializing (there are in fact many potential ones), but so far, they haven't moved the needle long term.

My thesis is as follows.

Thesis

  • Boston Omaha has many investors convinced of its fundamental value found in attractive subsidiaries and holdings, which are performing very well.
  • However, I believe this is a good example of where the market simply will not value a stock as "you want" due to management complications and lack of clarity. The company has been on a new journey since mid-2024, but I fail to see any near-term catalysts that could change the share price to anywhere positive where I could make a good return, buffeted by good safety.
  • I, therefore, say that BOC is a "HOLD" and give the company a share price of $11/share, about 30% below the current lowest analyst share price out there (from S&P Global).

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is qualitative
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 2 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 2/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $11/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2025.
